h2h_summary: England lead the all-time head-to-head with 5 wins to Croatia's 3, but Croatia have delivered famous victories in the biggest moments including the 2018 World Cup semi-final.
team_style_home: Under Thomas Tuchel, England play a possession-dominant 4-2-3-1 / 4-3-3 that morphs into a 3-2-5 in build-up, with inverted full-backs, aggressive counter-pressing, and over 70% average possession recorded throughout qualifying.
team_style_away: Croatia under Zlatko Dalic favour a structured 4-3-3 or 3-4-2-1 system centred on midfield control and technical quality, using disciplined possession to dictate tempo and relying on quick transitions and set-piece threat.
wc_history_home: England won the 1966 World Cup on home soil — their only major international title — and have since reached the semi-finals in 1990 and 2018, arriving at 2026 ranked 4th in the world seeking a second world title.
wc_history_away: Croatia have collected three World Cup medals since their 1998 debut — third place in 1998 and 2022, and runners-up in 2018 — making them one of the most consistently successful small nations in tournament history.
group_context: Group L features England, Croatia, Ghana, and Panama, with England and Croatia widely regarded as the two clear favourites to advance; the opening head-to-head clash on June 17 is likely to define which team progresses as group winners and sets the knockout bracket path.
strengths_home: World-class attacking trio in Kane, Bellingham, and Saka capable of unlocking any defence
Dominant possession game averaging over 70% throughout qualifying under Tuchel
Defensive solidity anchored by Rice and Guehi with a well-organised high-press system
strengths_away: Elite midfield intelligence and technical quality led by Modric and Kovacic
Experienced tournament mentality — three World Cup medals and a history of overperforming expectations
Strong defensive structure anchored by Gvardiol, one of Europe's best centre-backs
